topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of my mother’s lavender soap, once a comforting blanket, now felt suffocating. Every time she hummed while tending to the garden, a tight knot formed in my chest. I watched her laugh—a bright, unguarded sound—at something my father had just said. It wasn't a malicious feeling, exactly, just a sharp, unwelcome tightening in my gut, like a stone lodged beneath my ribs. I retreated to my room, the silence of the hallway amplifying the sound of their easy companionship. I picked at a loose thread on my sweater, my jaw clenched so tightly my temples throbbed. I needed space, a clear boundary, but the walls of this house felt too close, too shared.

[story 2]

"Oh, look at this, Mom! I finally got the recipe right," my brother exclaimed, holding up a steaming bowl. The warmth radiating off the ceramic seemed to mock the coolness settling over me. I forced a smile, the muscles in my face feeling stiff and unnatural. I made sure my voice was level, carefully neutral, while my eyes tracked the way my mother leaned in to listen, her attention completely focused on him. I busied myself rearranging books on my shelf, pushing them back into perfect alignment, a small, compulsive act of control. A strange heat bloomed behind my eyes, a restless energy that made my hands tremble slightly as I smoothed down the spine of a novel, desperate to feel something other than this prickling sensation.

[story 3]

I found myself hovering near the kitchen doorway, pretending to be engrossed in my phone, but truly, I was observing. My parents were sharing a quiet moment over coffee, their shoulders brushing in a familiar, comfortable rhythm. The easy affection between them felt like a physical weight pressing down on my own solitude. I took a slow, deliberate sip of my water, the coolness doing nothing to quell the rising tide of unease. When my father reached across the table to pat my mother’s hand, a sudden, sharp clenching seized my own fist. I subtly shifted my weight, creating a small distance between myself and the shared space, wishing desperately that I could simply dissolve into the wallpaper.
